subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Balance of Power Between the President and Congress
stimulus: The power of the executive branch in relation to the legislative branch has varied over time. Develop an argument that takes a position on the appropriate balance of power between the president and Congress.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Declaration of Independence, The Federalist 51, The Federalist 70.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The power of the executive should be strengthened relative to Congress because Congress is slow to react and sometimes ends up in gridlock.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The power of Congress should be strengthened relative to the president because Congress is the representative of the will of the people.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The power of the executive and legislative branches should be equal because no one branch should become too powerful.

wrong_examples: The power of the executive and legislative branches of government are important because there is a balance of power. (Does not respond to the prompt — does not take a position on who should have MORE power or whether the balance is correct)
The president and Congress both have important roles. (Vague — does not establish a line of reasoning or a defensible claim)
common_traps: The thesis must take a CLEAR POSITION — 'both are important' or 'it depends' does not earn Row A; must state a view on the appropriate balance
Foundational documents are NOT required in the thesis — do not deduct points for a thesis that does not cite the Declaration, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt (does not need to support the thesis).

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, Federalist 70, the War Powers Act, executive orders, the veto power, or another relevant course concept.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence with no connection to executive-legislative power dynamics
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A and does NOT require supporting the thesis — merely relevant evidence earns 1 point
Even an inaccurate characterization of a foundational document can earn Tier 1 if the document is relevant to the topic

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece of evidence that directly supports the thesis, OR two pieces of relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Federalist 51's argument about ambition counteracting ambition to support the thesis that branches should have equal power.

wrong_examples: Names two documents (e.g., 'Federalist 51 and Federalist 70') but does not explain how either supports the thesis
common_traps: Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A — no thesis means evidence is capped at 1 point
Simply naming a foundational document does not satisfy Tier 2 — must explain how the evidence connects to the claim

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 70's argument for a vigorous executive to support a stronger presidency thesis AND cites the War Powers Act as evidence of congressional attempts to check presidential war powers — both supporting the argument about appropriate balance.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from the listed documents (e.g., cites the veto power and executive orders without referencing Declaration, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70)
Uses a listed document but misattributes its argument (e.g., claims Federalist 70 argues for a weak executive — it argues for a strong one)
common_traps: Federalist 70 argues for a STRONG, energetic executive (Hamilton's view); Federalist 51 argues for separation of powers and checks and balances (Madison's view) — do not confuse their arguments
The Declaration of Independence is about tyranny and natural rights — it can support arguments for limited executive power or balance, but students must explain the connection

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: The Declaration of Independence expressed concern that the king had too much power, and therefore a stronger legislative branch accountable to the people would be needed to ensure a constitutional government that respects civil liberty.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist 70 argued that a strong executive is needed to act to protect the nation, which sometimes means the executive branch needs more authority to respond quickly to crises without waiting for congressional deliberation.

wrong_examples: Federalist 51 says there should be checks and balances. (Restates the evidence without explaining the causal link to the thesis)
The evidence I provided shows the balance of power is important. (Does not explain how or why the evidence supports the specific position taken)
common_traps: Reasoning requires a causal link: evidence → because/therefore → thesis; simply restating evidence is not reasoning
Requires Row A AND at least 2 evidence points (Row B ≥ 2) — scoring is conditional

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it — not merely acknowledge it exists.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While some say the president should be more powerful than Congress because of national security concerns which require immediate action, a more powerful legislature would ensure that those responses are in line with the will of the people.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While some say that the legislative branch should be more powerful than the executive branch because the legislature is closer to the will of the people, gridlock in the legislative branch could make fast action more difficult in times of national crisis.

wrong_examples: Many believe the president should be more powerful than Congress. (Only identifies the alternate perspective — no rebuttal, refutation, or concession)
Some people disagree with my argument. (Acknowledges opposition without describing or engaging with it)
common_traps: Must do TWO things: (1) describe the alternate perspective and (2) respond to it with reasoning — doing only one of these does not earn Row D
A concession is also acceptable — e.g., 'While I acknowledge that the president must sometimes act quickly, this does not outweigh the importance of legislative accountability'
